mappings.rationaleAI: Social domain of production, distribution, and consumption relevant to markets as an economic institution (C) and the price system in capitalist economies (D).
Private ownership with market competition constituting the market economy framework (C) and the price system as a means of organizing economic activity (D.1).
Foundational model of price determination addressing scarcity, utility, and value (A), equilibrium between supply and effective demand (C.6), and the price system determining what is produced (D.1).
Organized profit-seeking activity influencing seller behavior under competitive conditions (C.1.b) and price-fixing through business control (D.2.a).
Individual decision-making covering scarcity and utility (A), competitive versus monopolistic markets (C.1.a), and price determination across market structures (D).
Financial intermediary accepting deposits and extending loans, directly constituting markets for money and capital (C.2.c).
Social science analyzing markets, pricing, and distribution, covering value theory (A), market institutions (C), and price system analysis (D).
Aggregate economic analysis with aggregate supply-aggregate demand extending the equilibrium model, relevant to market equilibrium between supply and demand (C.6).
Critique of commodity fetishism and surplus value addressing utility and value (A), markets under socialism (C.3), and limitations of the price system (D.2).
Final-stage sale of goods to consumers constituting markets for manufactured goods (C.2.b), with pricing strategies and consumer behavior influencing spending (B.2).
Intangible economic output for which consumers pay, relevant to the market for labor and services (C.2.d).
Mandatory government levy relevant to government-established price controls and subsidies (D.2.b).
Productive activity functioning as a factor of production, relevant to the market for labor and services (C.2.d).
Processes of acquiring and retaining customers, directly constituting factors influencing consumers' tastes and spending (B.2).
Worker organization negotiating wages and conditions, relevant to the market for labor and services (C.2.d).
Legally enforceable rights including real and intellectual property, relevant to markets for primary commodities (C.2.a).
Generally accepted medium of exchange directly constituting the market for money and capital (C.2.c).
Risk-management mechanism constituting part of the securities and capital market (C.2.c).
Transfer of goods and services through market networks (C), with international trade aspects covered by markets in international trade (C.5).
Social ownership of production constituting the counterpart of the market under full-scale economic planning (C.3).
Price mechanisms and the invisible hand addressing scarcity, utility, and value (A), the market in economic theory (C.4), and the price system organizing economic activity (D.1).
Cultural practices and art forms produced through mass media and marketing, directly constituting factors influencing consumers' tastes and spending (B.2).
Administration of organizations through strategic planning and resource control, relevant to influences affecting seller behavior under competitive conditions (C.1.b).
Supranational union with single market and customs union, relevant to government regulation of monopolistic practices (C.1.d) and markets in international trade (C.5).
Collective ownership ideology relevant to markets under socialism and planned economy alternatives (C.3).
Legal entity with board governance relevant to influences affecting seller behavior under various competitive conditions (C.1.b).
Movement of people relevant to labor market dynamics and the market for labor and services (C.2.d).
Government-funded support systems relevant to government-established price controls and subsidies (D.2.b).
Harmful contaminants from human activities constituting externalities such as pollution not susceptible to price control (D.2.c).
Transportation infrastructure relevant to externalities such as highway congestion not susceptible to price control (D.2.c).
Universal consumer good with fashion influencing tastes and spending (B.2), traded in markets for manufactured goods (C.2.b).
Intensifying economic interdependence driving international trade and the emergence of global markets (C.5).
Machine for transportation constituting a major category of manufactured goods market (C.2.b).
Movement systems relevant to markets for manufactured goods (C.2.b) and externalities such as congestion (D.2.c).
